title: Universal Chow group of 0-cycle and nilpotence
speaker: Claire Voisin

proof_stages.label: Chow decomposition and universal CH0
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a smooth projective complex variety and defines an integral Chow decomposition of the diagonal, then proves its equivalence with universal triviality of CH0 by using correspondences, the generic point over the function field, and localization.

proof_stages.label: Stable birational motivation
proof_stages.summary: The decomposition-of-the-diagonal property is shown to be stably birationally invariant, using projective-space factors and reduction of a birational map to degree-one morphisms after resolution/blow-up.

proof_stages.label: Bloch–Srinivas rational-coefficient origin
proof_stages.summary: The Bloch–Srinivas theorem is recalled: over a large algebraically closed field, CH0(X)=Z is equivalent to a decomposition of the diagonal with rational coefficients, proved through a generic-point argument plus torsion/transfer and Roitman’s theorem.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological decomposition and topological obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A homological/cohomological version of diagonal decomposition is introduced, and its action on integral cohomology is used to prove that torsion in H3 and H2 must vanish, yielding topological obstructions to integral Chow decomposition.

proof_stages.label: Main integral comparison problem
proof_stages.summary: After noting higher unramified cohomology obstructions to Chow decomposition, the central question is posed: whether a smooth projective variety can have an integral cohomological decomposition of the diagonal but no Chow decomposition.

proof_stages.label: Rational-coefficient nilpotence reduction
proof_stages.summary: For Q-coefficients, the nilpotence conjecture for homologically trivial correspondences would imply that any cohomological diagonal decomposition can be powered to a Chow decomposition, so the rational-coefficient comparison should be positive.

proof_stages.label: Integral nilpotence from algebraic equivalence
proof_stages.summary: The integral analogue of nilpotence is discussed via the Voevodsky/Voisin theorem that algebraically trivial cycles are smash-nilpotent, with proof reduced to degree-zero 0-cycles on curves and the geometry of symmetric products and Jacobians.

proof_stages.label: Surface consequence and counterexample strategy
proof_stages.summary: As an application, complex surfaces with CH0=Z have a Chow decomposition exactly when they have a cohomological one, while known homologically trivial non-smash-nilpotent integral cycles motivate searching for varieties with torsion-free algebraic cohomology but nonzero unramified cohomology.

examples.name: Artin–Mumford unirational double solid
examples.note: The Artin–Mumford nodal quartic double cover of projective 3-space is used to illustrate how nontrivial torsion in cohomology obstructs a cohomological, hence Chow, decomposition of the diagonal despite trivial CH0.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker invokes the Artin–Mumford unirational double solid as a double cover of P3 with nontrivial Brauer/torsion H3 and applies the obstruction lemma to conclude it has no homological or Chow decomposition of the diagonal, hence is not stably rational

passes.reason: Used as a unirational/rationally connected double solid with nontrivial Artin–Mumford invariant showing trivial CH0 need not be universally trivial and obstructing stable rationality, but it is not a sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.

Used as a unirational/rationally connected double solid with nontrivial Brauer torsion, obstructing homological/Chow decomposition of the diagonal and stable rationality, while not being a structurally degenerate, extremal, symmetric, or sporadic exceptional variety.

The Artin–Mumford double solid is a nodal degeneration of a smooth quartic double solid, used here as a unirational/CH0-trivial variety whose torsion Brauer/H^3 class obstructs homological and Chow diagonal decompositions.

The Artin–Mumford double solid is a non-sporadic nodal quartic double-cover example, used here to show that unirational/rationally connected varieties can have trivial CH0 but no integral Chow or homological decomposition of the diagonal.
Used as a unirational/rationally connected double solid with trivial CH0 but nontrivial torsion obstruction, hence no homological/Chow decomposition of the diagonal and not stably rational.

It is an otherwise non-sporadic nodal quartic double-solid example used to show that unirational/rationally connected varieties with trivial CH0 can still lack universal CH0-triviality and a Chow decomposition of the diagonal.

A nodal quartic double solid from the Artin–Mumford construction is used to show that unirational/rationally connected varieties can have CH0=Z but nontrivial Brauer torsion, hence no universal CH0-triviality or Chow decomposition of the diagonal.

Used to show that a unirational/rationally connected variety can have CH0=Z but fail universal CH0-triviality and have no Chow decomposition of the diagonal; structurally it is a special but non-sporadic double-solid construction, not degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.
a nodal quartic double solid/resolution used to show a unirational, CH0-trivial variety can fail universal CH0-triviality and stable rationality

It is used to show that unirational/rationally connected varieties can have CH0=Z yet fail universal CH0-triviality/diagonal decomposition via nontrivial torsion Brauer/H^3, while structurally it is a special family member rather than symmetric, sporadic, extremal, or degenerate.

Used to exhibit a unirational/rationally connected threefold with nontrivial Brauer/H^3-torsion, hence trivial CH0 but no universal CH0-triviality or Chow decomposition of the diagonal, while the double solid itself is not a maximally symmetric, extremal, or sporadic classification exception.

It is used as a unirational/rationally connected threefold with nontrivial Brauer torsion showing universal CH0-triviality and Chow/homological diagonal decomposition can fail, while structurally it is a member of a family rather than a sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.
